title: The Canterbury Tales. The Shipman's Tale. by Geoffrey Chaucer — Sad Poetry Lines
content:
THE PROLOGUE

Our Host upon his stirrups stood anon,
And saide; "Good men, hearken every one,
This was a thrifty tale for the nones.
Sir Parish Priest," quoth he, "for Godde's bones,
Tell us a tale, as was thy forword yore:
I see well that ye learned men in lore
meaning: "The Canterbury Tales. The Shipman's Tale." by Geoffrey Chaucer is a sad and love and deep and nature and inspirational poem consisting of 482 lines. The poet explores themes of sad and love and deep and nature and inspirational through vivid imagery and emotional depth. Beginning with "THE PROLOGUE ...", this piece captures the essence of human experience and invites reflection on the deeper currents of life and feeling. This work is part of Geoffrey Chaucer's celebrated body of poetry that continues to resonate with readers worldwide.
